name: Bhaskararaya
dates_notes: Early-modern Vārāṇasī Śrīvidyā theologian; dated by manuscript colophons of the Saubhāgya-Bhāskara (composed ca. 1729) and the Setubandha (composed ca. 1733).
core_thesis: Bhāskararāya is the major early-modern theologian of the *Śrīvidyā* tradition — the Śākta lineage centered on the goddess Tripurā-Sundarī, the *śrī-cakra*, and the recitation of the *Lalitā-Sahasranāma*. Working in Vārāṇasī in the early eighteenth century, he writes *Saubhāgya-Bhāskara*, *Setubandha*, and the *Varivasyā-Rahasya* with *Prakāśa* — *vyākhyāna* and *prakāśa*-gloss in the colophons — and within them states the goddess's identity with Brahman: *Cit-Śakti* *non-different* from Brahman in essence yet expressing herself as the entire manifest universe through her own *svātantrya* (*Saubhāgya-Bhāskara* on *Lalitā-Sahasranāma* names *cit*, *citi*, *cidānanda-rūpā*; [*Varivasyā-Rahasya* 1.1–10](cite://bhaskararaya/varivasya-rahasya/1.1-10), the foundational disposition). The *śrī-cakra* is read as the diagrammatic articulation of this *svātantrya*: the *bindu* at the center is *Cit*-Brahman, the surrounding *āvaraṇas* are the levels of manifestation, and the practitioner's progressive interiorisation of the *cakra* is the recognition of identity with the *Cit-Śakti* ([*Varivasyā-Rahasya* 2.1–35](cite://bhaskararaya/varivasya-rahasya/2.1-35) with *Prakāśa* on the *navāvaraṇa* sequence; *Setubandha* on *Nityā-Ṣoḍaśikārṇava* 1.1–20). Bhāskararāya is doctrinally aligned with the *kaula* rather than the *samaya* sub-tradition of Śrīvidyā — he defends the legitimacy of the full *pañca-makāra* ritual practice against more conservative Smārta-aligned readings (*Setubandha* on *NṢA* 5; *Saubhāgya-Bhāskara* on *LSN* names *kaula-mārga-tatparā*, *kaulinī*) — but works within an Advaitic metaphysical frame. The position is not eclectic: Brahman *as* Tripurā-Sundarī, not Brahman *and* Tripurā-Sundarī as separable; the goddess is not symbol but the active mode of the supreme Reality (*Saubhāgya-Bhāskara* on *LSN* names *brahma-rūpā*, *brahmātmaikya-svarūpiṇī*).

engaged_works.title: Saubhagya-Bhaskara
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on the Lalitā-Sahasranāma.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus; the Saubhāgya-Bhāskara is the standard Śrīvidyā commentary on the Lalitā-Sahasranāma in the Sanskrit tradition.

engaged_works.summary: The standard Śrīvidyā commentary on the *Lalitā-Sahasranāma* — the thousand-name hymn to the goddess Lalitā Tripurā-Sundarī from the *Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa*'s *Lalitopākhyāna*. Bhāskararāya works through each name as a doctrinally-saturated technical term, drawing simultaneously on Śrīvidyā Tantric scripture — *Vāmakeśvara-Tantra*, *Tantra-Rāja-Tantra*, *Yoginī-Hṛdaya* (cited throughout, esp. on the *śrī-cakra*-related names) — on Advaita Vedānta sources — *Brahma-Sūtras*, the principal *Upaniṣads*, Śaṅkara's *Bhāṣyas* (cited on the *brahma-rūpā* name-set, *Saubhāgya-Bhāskara* ad *LSN* names *cit*, *cidānanda-rūpā*) — and on the *śrī-cakra* iconography that the names presuppose (*SB* on the name-set *cakrarājanilayā* through *bindu-trikoṇa-gā*). The integration is precise: each name is read so as to articulate, in liturgical voice, an aspect of the goddess's identity with *Cit*-Brahman and her free self-expression as the manifest universe (*SB* on *LSN* names *brahmātmaikya-svarūpiṇī*, *sarva-vedānta-saṃvedyā*). The commentary is the standard reference for *Lalitā-Sahasranāma* recitation in Smārta and Śrīvidyā traditions to the present day, and is the canonical document of Bhāskararāya's reading.

engaged_works.title: Setubandha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on the Nityā-ṣoḍaśikārṇava (the first part of the Vāmakeśvara-Tantra).
engaged_works.summary: The major commentary on the *Nityā-Ṣoḍaśikārṇava* — the first part of the *Vāmakeśvara-Tantra*, one of the principal scriptures of the *Śrīvidyā* tradition. The *Nityā-Ṣoḍaśikārṇava* sets out the doctrine of the sixteen *nityā* deities surrounding Tripurā-Sundarī in the *śrī-cakra* (*NṢA* 1.1–60 with *Setubandha* ad loc.), the *pañcadaśākṣarī* and *ṣoḍaśākṣarī* mantras (*NṢA* 1.92–110, *Setubandha* on the syllable-by-syllable analysis), and the ritual procedures of *śrī-vidyā* worship (*NṢA* 2–4, *Setubandha* on the *navāvaraṇa-pūjā*). Bhāskararāya's *Setubandha* — the "bridge-building" commentary — articulates the doctrinal and ritual content systematically and within an Advaita-aligned theological frame: the sixteen *nityās* are read as the differentiated self-expressions of the single supreme *Cit-Śakti* (*Setubandha* on *NṢA* 1.13–35), and the *pañcadaśākṣarī* mantra as the phonemic articulation of her *svātantrya* (*Setubandha* on *NṢA* 1.95–110). The work is the school's standard Tantra-commentary and is the principal source for understanding *Śrīvidyā* ritual at the level of doctrinal commentary rather than at the level of practical *paddhati*.

engaged_works.title: Varivasya-Rahasya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored independent treatise on Śrīvidyā worship; with Bhāskararāya's own auto-commentary, the Prakāśa.
engaged_works.summary: An independent verse-treatise (with Bhāskararāya's own *Prakāśa* commentary) devoted to the inner *rahasya* of *varivasyā* — the secret meaning of *Śrīvidyā* worship. Where the *Saubhāgya-Bhāskara* and *Setubandha* are commentaries on received scriptures, the *Varivasyā-Rahasya* is Bhāskararāya's own systematic statement of the school's doctrine. The treatise unfolds the *pañcadaśākṣarī* mantra — the fifteen-syllable *kādi-vidyā* — syllable by syllable as the phonemic articulation of the *Cit-Śakti*'s self-expression ([*Varivasyā-Rahasya* 1.11–80](cite://bhaskararaya/varivasya-rahasya/1.11-80) with *Prakāśa*, the syllable-by-syllable analysis of the *vāgbhava*, *kāmarāja*, and *śakti* *kūṭas*); gives the *bhāvanā* (meditative interiorisation) of the *śrī-cakra* as the practitioner's own progressive recognition of identity with the goddess (*VR* 2.1–40 with *Prakāśa* on the *navāvaraṇa-bhāvanā*); and articulates the integration of *mantra-japa*, *yantra-pūjā*, and *bhāvanā* as the threefold structure of *Śrīvidyā* practice (*VR* 2.50–end). The work is the school's most concentrated single-volume statement and is the natural starting point for any structural study of early-modern Śrīvidyā theology in its Advaita-aligned form.

reception_notes: Bhāskararāya is the canonical early-modern Śrīvidyā theologian; his commentaries are the standard references for the school's living tradition. The Saubhāgya-Bhāskara is the standard commentary on the Lalitā-Sahasranāma in Smārta and Śrīvidyā recitation contexts to the present day. Modern scholarship (Brooks, Padoux, Sanderson) has placed Bhāskararāya at the center of the early-modern Śākta-Vedānta synthesis, distinguishing his Vārāṇasī Advaita-aligned line from the more conservative Smārta-aligned line of Lakṣmīdhara.
